text: We present a robotic repair of a complex colovesical fistula with chronic bladder wall abscess due to diverticulitis. Our patient is an eighty-two-year-old woman with a history of diverticulitis. She presented with recurrent urinary tract infections and intermittent pneumaturia. On exam, her vitals were within normal limits and her abdomen was soft and non-tender. CT scans showed air tracking from the sigmoid colon to the bladder consistent with the colovesical fistula. with associated chronic bladder wall abscess. Cystoscopy noted no evidence of malignancy and identified the fistula. Preoperative colonoscopy showed benign stricture and no evidence of malignancy. A representative CT image is shown with the arrow marking the fistula.

The operating room setup is displayed, with the patient cart positioned on the patient's left side. Three working ports are visibly labeled, and the assistant stands on the patient's right, utilizing one of the labeled ports.

The surgeon performs central ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and vein (IMV) using a medial to lateral approach. The inferior mesenteric vein is ligated near the ligament of Treitz and below the pancreas, followed by ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery close to its origin. The dissection continues, developing the plane between the colon mesentery and Gerota's fascia, with careful identification and preservation of the ureters. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized.

The pedicle ligation and splenic flexure mobilization are completed. The robotic arm is repositioned from the right upper quadrant to the left midabdomen, and the robot is redirected to the pelvis to address the colovesical fistula. The new working and assistant ports are labeled. The rectosigmoid colon is visibly densely adherent to the bladder.
The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to separate the sigmoid colon from the fibrotic bladder abscess cavity and fistula tract.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the sigmoid, rectosigmoid, and upper rectum. The mesorectum is ligated and divided, and a healthy segment of the rectum is stapled and divided. The fibrotic and thick-walled bladder wall abscess is resected along with the fistula tract. A cystotomy is performed to identify the trigone and facilitate the resection of the bladder wall.
The surgeon closes the bladder resection and cystotomy sites using a running suture technique in two layers. An end-to-end colorectal anastomosis is performed, and a drain is placed in the pelvis.

Pathology showed inflamed bladder and colon tissue without evidence of malignancy. The Foley catheter was removed on post-update twelve after a CT cystogram showed no evidence of a leak. This is an example of where the robotic platform facilitated a minimally invasive repair of a complex colovesical fistula.
